title: The Resolved Brief
subtitle: Everything Your Family Needs to Know. All in One Place.
design_notes: Every follow_up_tip should reinforce that answering the question IS the solution. The brief documents it, the Vault Page secures it. Minimize homework. Where follow-up IS needed, provide the specific resource, link, or 5-minute action.

sections.title: Quick Snapshot
sections.subtitle: Let's see where you stand — just tap through these. No typing needed.
sections.intro_message: We'll start with a quick pulse check. These yes/no questions take about 60 seconds and help us tailor the rest of the walkthrough to your situation.

questions.prompt: Do you have a will?
questions.follow_up_tip: If not — no stress. FreeWill.com and Trust & Will let you create a basic will online in about 20 minutes for free or under $100. We'll add this to your brief so you know exactly where to go.

questions.prompt: When was your will last updated?
questions.follow_up_tip: If it's been more than 3 years, a quick review with your attorney is usually 30 minutes and under $200. We'll flag this in your brief.

questions.prompt: Have there been major life changes since then — marriage, divorce, children, property, inheritance?
questions.follow_up_tip: If yes, your will may not reflect your current wishes — but updating is usually quick. We'll note this in your brief so it doesn't fall through the cracks.

questions.prompt: Do you have life insurance?
questions.follow_up_tip: If yes — great, we'll document it so your family knows exactly where to file a claim. If no, we'll include a note in your brief. Term life policies start around $20-30/month for most people.

questions.prompt: Do you have a health care proxy or power of attorney?
questions.follow_up_tip: If not, your state has a free form for this. We'll include the right link in your brief — it takes about 10 minutes to fill out and doesn't require a lawyer.

questions.prompt: Do you use a password manager?
questions.follow_up_tip: If yes, perfect — we just need to know which one so your family knows where to look. If no, don't worry — your Vault Page handles this. You'll write your key passwords by hand and seal them in an envelope.

questions.prompt: Do you have a living will or advance directive?
questions.follow_up_tip: If not — we'll capture your wishes (resuscitation, organ donation) right here in this walkthrough. Your brief will document them clearly so your family and doctors know what you want.

questions.prompt: How many bank accounts do you have?
questions.follow_up_tip: We'll list every one in your brief so your family knows exactly where to look. No account numbers needed — just the bank names.

questions.prompt: Are your important documents organized in one place?
questions.follow_up_tip: After this walkthrough, they will be. That's what the Resolved Brief is — one document with everything organized.

questions.prompt: Could your family find your insurance policies if they needed to?
questions.follow_up_tip: By the time we're done, your brief will list every policy, provider, and where the documents are. Your family won't need to dig.

questions.prompt: Are most of your bills on autopay?
questions.follow_up_tip: We'll document which bills are on autopay and which aren't — so if something happens, your family knows what keeps running and what needs manual attention.

questions.prompt: Do you have any retirement accounts?
questions.follow_up_tip: We'll capture where they're held so your family knows they exist. Billions in retirement funds go unclaimed every year simply because nobody knew to look.

questions.prompt: Have you told anyone your final wishes?
questions.follow_up_tip: By the end of this walkthrough, your wishes will be clearly documented in your brief. That conversation becomes a lot easier when you can just hand someone the document.

questions.prompt: Does anyone besides you know where your passwords are?
questions.follow_up_tip: Your Resolved Brief includes a Vault Page — you'll write your key passwords by hand, seal it in an envelope, and keep it somewhere safe. No digital storage, no risk.

sections.title: Money & Accounts
sections.subtitle: Where your money lives. Just names and account types — no account numbers.
sections.intro_message: This is the section that prevents your family from going on a scavenger hunt. Just tell us where the money is — we'll organize it into a clear reference they can use.

questions.prompt: Which banks or credit unions do you use?
questions.placeholder: e.g., Chase, Bank of America, local credit union...
questions.follow_up_tip: Just the names — your brief will organize these into a clean list your family can reference immediately. Account numbers go on your Vault Page later.

questions.prompt: Types of accounts you have?
questions.follow_up_tip: This helps your family understand the full picture. Joint accounts are accessible immediately — we'll note that in your brief.

questions.prompt: Anyone else listed on these accounts?
questions.placeholder: e.g., spouse, business partner...
questions.follow_up_tip: Joint account holders can access funds right away without any legal process. This is one of the most important details in your entire brief.

questions.prompt: What retirement accounts do you have?
questions.follow_up_tip: Each of these will be listed in your brief with the institution name — that's all your family needs to start the beneficiary claim process.

questions.prompt: Where are they held?
questions.placeholder: e.g., Fidelity, Vanguard, through employer...
questions.follow_up_tip: The institution name is everything. With it, your family can call and start the process. Without it, they wouldn't even know to look.

questions.prompt: Any investment or brokerage accounts outside retirement?
questions.placeholder: e.g., Schwab brokerage, Robinhood, real estate holdings...
questions.follow_up_tip: These are the accounts that get forgotten most often. If nobody knows it exists, nobody can claim it.

questions.prompt: Mortgage or rent — who do you pay?
questions.placeholder: e.g., Wells Fargo mortgage, Greystar property mgmt...
questions.follow_up_tip: This goes straight into your brief. If something happens, this is the first bill your family needs to handle — now they'll know exactly who to call.

questions.prompt: Any loans? (car, student, personal, business)
questions.placeholder: e.g., Toyota Financial, Navient student loans...
questions.follow_up_tip: Good to know: many loans have life insurance or death discharge provisions. Your family should ask each lender directly — we'll note that in your brief.

questions.prompt: Credit cards you use?
questions.placeholder: e.g., Chase Sapphire, Amex, Discover...
questions.follow_up_tip: Your family will need to close or transfer these. Having the list ready saves them from hunting through mail and statements.

questions.prompt: Where are your financial documents kept?
questions.follow_up_tip: Whatever you choose, your brief will document it so your family knows exactly where to look first.

questions.prompt: Do you have a safe deposit box?
questions.follow_up_tip: If yes, we'll include the bank and key location in your brief. Without that info, accessing a safe deposit box can require a court order.

questions.prompt: Which bank, and who has the key?
questions.placeholder: e.g., Chase Main St branch, spare key in desk drawer
questions.follow_up_tip: This is now documented. One less thing your family has to figure out under pressure.

sections.title: Income & Bills
sections.subtitle: Where money comes in and where it goes out.
sections.intro_message: Bills don't stop when life does. This section maps what's coming in and going out so your family can keep things running without scrambling.

questions.prompt: What is your primary bank account — the one most of your money flows through?
questions.placeholder: e.g., Chase checking
questions.follow_up_tip: This is ground zero for your family's financial continuity. It goes right at the top of your brief.

questions.prompt: Any other accounts your family should know about?
questions.placeholder: e.g., separate savings, business account, HSA...
questions.follow_up_tip: Hidden accounts are one of the biggest surprises families face. By listing them here, you're preventing that.

questions.prompt: Where does your income come from?
questions.follow_up_tip: Your brief will note each source and what your family may need to do — contact employer HR, notify Social Security, reach out to tenants, etc. We'll spell it out for them.

questions.prompt: Bills on autopay?
questions.placeholder: e.g., mortgage, electric, internet, car insurance...
questions.follow_up_tip: Autopay keeps things running — but your family needs to know which card it's on. If that card gets frozen, these all stop. Your brief will flag this.

questions.prompt: Bills you pay manually?
questions.placeholder: e.g., water bill, property taxes...
questions.follow_up_tip: These are the ones that get missed first. Documenting them here means your family won't find out about them via a late notice.

questions.prompt: Subscriptions and memberships?
questions.placeholder: e.g., Netflix, gym, Amazon Prime, software...
questions.follow_up_tip: Your family will know exactly what to cancel and what to keep. No more charges slipping through for months.

questions.prompt: Annual payments that could be missed?
questions.placeholder: e.g., property tax, HOA, life insurance premium, car registration...
questions.follow_up_tip: These are the sneaky ones — they only come once a year and they're easy to miss. Your brief will list them with approximate timing so nothing slips.

sections.title: Insurance & Benefits
sections.subtitle: Your safety net — what's covered and where the policies are.
sections.intro_message: Insurance only works if someone files a claim. This section makes sure your family knows what policies exist, who to call, and where to find the paperwork.

questions.prompt: Do you have life insurance?
questions.follow_up_tip: Your brief will include the provider, coverage amount, and beneficiary — everything your family needs to file the claim. No digging required.

questions.prompt: Provider(s)?
questions.placeholder: e.g., MetLife through employer, Northwestern Mutual private...
questions.follow_up_tip: With just the provider name, your family can call and start the claims process the same day.

questions.prompt: Approximate coverage amount?
questions.placeholder: e.g., $500K through work + $250K private
questions.follow_up_tip: Even a rough number helps your family understand what's coming and plan accordingly.

questions.prompt: Who is the beneficiary?
questions.placeholder: e.g., spouse, children, trust...
questions.follow_up_tip: This is documented now. If it needs updating, your brief's follow-up section will remind you — most providers let you change it online in 5 minutes.

questions.prompt: Disability insurance?
questions.follow_up_tip: If yes, it's in your brief. If no or not sure — check with your employer's HR. Many people have it through work and don't realize it.

questions.prompt: Long-term care insurance?
questions.follow_up_tip: If yes, we'll document it. If no, that's fine — this is something to consider down the road, not today.

questions.prompt: Home or renter's insurance?
questions.placeholder: e.g., State Farm, policy # in filing cabinet...
questions.follow_up_tip: Your brief will list the provider so your family can file property claims immediately if needed.

questions.prompt: Auto insurance?
questions.placeholder: e.g., Geico, Progressive...
questions.follow_up_tip: Needs to stay active on all vehicles. Your family will know who to call to keep coverage running.

questions.prompt: Any other policies? (umbrella, business, liability)
questions.placeholder: e.g., $1M umbrella through State Farm...
questions.follow_up_tip: These are especially hard to find after the fact. Documenting them now means they won't get missed.

questions.prompt: Where are your insurance documents?
questions.follow_up_tip: Whatever the answer, it's now in your brief. If it's 'not sure' — your agent can usually pull everything with one phone call. We'll include their info.

questions.prompt: Insurance agent or broker?
questions.placeholder: e.g., John Smith, State Farm, 555-0123...
questions.follow_up_tip: This person is your family's single best resource. One call to them can unlock access to all your policies at once.

sections.title: Digital Life & Access
sections.subtitle: How your family gets into your accounts when they need to.
sections.intro_message: Your entire life is behind a passcode. This section makes sure your family can get in when they need to — without storing anything sensitive digitally. That's what your Vault Page is for.

questions.prompt: How do you manage your passwords?
questions.follow_up_tip: Your brief will document your method so your family knows where to start. Your Vault Page is where you'll write the master password by hand — sealed in an envelope, never stored digitally.

questions.prompt: Which password manager do you use?
questions.follow_up_tip: Most password managers have an Emergency Access feature that lets a trusted person request access after a waiting period. Your brief will note whether yours does and how to set it up — it takes about 5 minutes.

questions.prompt: Where will the master password or access method be stored for your family?
questions.follow_up_tip: The Vault Page in your Resolved Brief is designed for exactly this. You'll write it by hand, seal the page in an envelope, and keep it with your brief. Done.

questions.prompt: Primary email account?
questions.placeholder: e.g., yourname@gmail.com
questions.follow_up_tip: Email is the skeleton key to your digital life — password resets for almost everything go through it. This goes in your brief, and the password goes on your Vault Page.

questions.prompt: Could your family get into your phone if needed?
options: No — only I can unlock it
questions.follow_up_tip: If no — your Vault Page has a dedicated line for your phone passcode. Write it there. Your phone unlocks 2FA codes, banking apps, and everything else.

questions.prompt: Could your family get into your computer?
options: No — only I can access it
questions.follow_up_tip: If no — same as your phone. Add the computer password to your Vault Page. Tax documents, financial software, and saved files often live here.

questions.prompt: Financial apps on your phone?
questions.follow_up_tip: These are documented in your brief. Your family will know which apps to open once they have phone access.

questions.prompt: Do you have any cryptocurrency?
questions.follow_up_tip: If yes — this is critical. Crypto without a recovery phrase is gone forever. We'll capture where it's held next.

questions.prompt: Which platform, and where is the recovery phrase stored?
questions.placeholder: e.g., Coinbase, seed phrase in safe...
questions.follow_up_tip: Your brief will document the platform. The recovery phrase or seed words go on your Vault Page — written by hand, sealed in the envelope. Never digital.

questions.prompt: Where are your photos stored?
questions.follow_up_tip: Your brief will list every location. Photos are irreplaceable — your family will know exactly where to find them.

questions.prompt: Important documents in cloud storage?
questions.follow_up_tip: Tax returns, contracts, legal docs — your brief lists where they live. With your email and password access via the Vault Page, your family can reach everything.

sections.title: Medical & Emergency
sections.subtitle: Who makes decisions and what they need to know.
sections.intro_message: If you were in an ER and couldn't speak, this section makes sure the right person steps in — and they know exactly what you want. No guessing.

questions.prompt: Who should make medical decisions if you can't?
questions.placeholder: e.g., spouse, parent, sibling, friend...
questions.follow_up_tip: This person is now documented in your brief and on your Family Emergency Card. In a crisis, your family hands that card to the hospital and the question is answered.

questions.prompt: Does this person know they've been chosen?
questions.follow_up_tip: If not — your Resolved Brief makes that conversation easy. You can hand it to them and say 'I put this together for our family. You're listed as my healthcare proxy.' Five minutes, done.

questions.prompt: Backup person if they're unavailable?
questions.placeholder: e.g., sibling, close friend...
questions.follow_up_tip: Now you have a primary and a backup, both documented. Your family won't have to argue about who's in charge.

questions.prompt: Current medical conditions?
questions.placeholder: e.g., high blood pressure, diabetes, asthma...
questions.follow_up_tip: This goes directly onto your Family Emergency Card — the one-page reference your family brings to the ER.

questions.prompt: Current medications?
questions.placeholder: e.g., Lisinopril 10mg, Metformin 500mg...
questions.follow_up_tip: Include dosages if you know them. ER doctors need this immediately and your family often doesn't know it by heart.

questions.prompt: Allergies (medications, foods, other)?
questions.placeholder: e.g., penicillin, shellfish, latex...
questions.follow_up_tip: This can be life-or-death information. It's now on your Emergency Card, ready to hand to a doctor.

questions.prompt: Primary care doctor?
questions.placeholder: e.g., Dr. Smith, Northwestern Medical...
questions.follow_up_tip: Your PCP has your complete medical history. Your family will know who to call.

questions.prompt: Specialists you see regularly?
questions.placeholder: e.g., cardiologist Dr. Jones, therapist Dr. Lee...
questions.follow_up_tip: These get listed in your brief so your family can follow up with ongoing care or get medical records.

questions.prompt: Health insurance provider?
questions.placeholder: e.g., Blue Cross Blue Shield through employer...
questions.follow_up_tip: Hospital billing asks for this immediately. It's now on your Emergency Card.

questions.prompt: Where is your insurance card?
questions.follow_up_tip: Documented. Your family knows where to grab it. If it's on your phone, the Vault Page passcode gets them in.

questions.prompt: Do you have a living will or advance directive?
questions.follow_up_tip: If you don't have one yet — answering the next two questions captures your core wishes right now. Your brief will document them clearly. If you want to make it legally binding, your state's free advance directive form takes about 10 minutes. We'll include the link.

questions.prompt: Resuscitation preference?
options: Want to discuss with doctor first
questions.follow_up_tip: This is now documented. Your family won't have to make this decision for you in a hospital hallway. That alone is worth this entire walkthrough.

questions.prompt: Organ donor?
questions.follow_up_tip: Documented. Make sure this matches your driver's license — if they conflict, it creates confusion. Takes 2 minutes to update at your state's DMV website.

sections.title: Wishes & Final Instructions
sections.subtitle: The things only you can tell them.
sections.intro_message: This is the most personal section. There are no wrong answers. Whatever you share here, your family will be grateful they didn't have to guess.

questions.prompt: Do you prefer burial or cremation?
questions.follow_up_tip: Whatever you choose — even 'no preference' — is a gift to your family. It removes one of the hardest decisions they'd otherwise face while grieving.

questions.prompt: Any specific wishes about your remains?
questions.placeholder: e.g., family plot, scatter ashes at the lake, specific funeral home...
questions.follow_up_tip: This goes in your brief under Final Wishes. Your family will know exactly what you wanted.

questions.prompt: What kind of service would you want?
questions.follow_up_tip: Documented. Your family gets to focus on honoring you instead of debating what you would have wanted.

questions.prompt: Any specific requests?
questions.placeholder: e.g., play a certain song, specific readings, no flowers — donate instead...
questions.follow_up_tip: These details make the service feel like you. Your family will be glad they had this.

questions.prompt: Anyone who should be notified that your family might not think of?
questions.placeholder: e.g., old college friend, former colleague, online community...
questions.follow_up_tip: There are always people who slip through the cracks. Now they won't.

questions.prompt: Specific items for specific people?
questions.placeholder: e.g., grandmother's ring to daughter, guitar to best friend...
questions.follow_up_tip: This prevents the kind of conflict that breaks families apart. Your wishes are in writing — clear and settled.

questions.prompt: Anything to donate, sell, or destroy?
questions.placeholder: e.g., donate clothes to Goodwill, sell the boat, shred old journals...
questions.follow_up_tip: Clear permission to act. Your family won't feel guilty about handling things because you told them exactly what to do.

questions.prompt: Is there anything you want your family to know — messages, values, traditions, love you want to pass on?
questions.placeholder: This is your space. Say what matters most to you.
questions.follow_up_tip: This is the page your family will read over and over. Take your time.

follow_up_section.title: What's Left — And How To Finish It
follow_up_section.subtitle: Your Resolved Brief handles the heavy lifting. These are the few things that need one more step — with exactly how to do each one.
follow_up_section.design_note: This section is dynamically generated based on user answers. Only show items where a gap was identified. Each item includes the specific action AND the resource/link to complete it.

items.solution: FreeWill.com (free) or Trust & Will ($159) — takes about 20 minutes online. No lawyer needed for a basic will.

items.item: Set up a healthcare proxy / power of attorney
items.solution: Your state has a free form — search '[your state] advance directive form.' Fill it out, sign with a witness. No lawyer needed.

items.trigger: S5 = No AND Q11 = 'No — but I want one'
items.item: Create a living will / advance directive
items.solution: Same form as your healthcare proxy in most states. Your wishes are already captured in this brief — the form makes them legally binding.

items.trigger: Q35 != empty AND beneficiary may need updating
items.solution: Log into each financial account and check the beneficiary. Most can be updated online in 5 minutes per account.

items.item: Consolidate financial documents in one location
items.solution: Pick one spot — a filing cabinet, a safe, or a cloud folder. Move everything there. Your brief already lists what you have, so use it as your checklist.

items.trigger: Q16 = empty or no joint holder AND primary account exists
items.item: Consider joint access or POD (payable on death) on key accounts
items.solution: Call your bank and ask to add a POD beneficiary. This lets your family access funds immediately without probate. Most banks do it over the phone in 10 minutes.

items.trigger: Q44 is populated AND password manager has emergency access
items.item: Set up Emergency Access in your password manager
items.solution: 1Password: Settings → Emergency Kit. LastPass: Settings → Emergency Access. Bitwarden: Settings → Emergency Access. Add your trusted person — they can request access after a waiting period you set.

items.item: Back up photos to a second location
items.solution: Turn on iCloud Photos or Google Photos auto-backup. Free up to 15GB (Google) or 5GB (iCloud). Or plug in an external drive and drag your Photos folder over.
items.time_estimate: 10 minutes to set up, runs automatically

items.item: Enable Legacy Contact on Apple / Google accounts
items.solution: Apple: Settings → [Your Name] → Password & Security → Legacy Contact. Google: myaccount.google.com → Inactive Account Manager. Lets a trusted person access your account if something happens.

items.item: Tell your healthcare proxy they've been chosen
items.solution: Hand them your Resolved Brief and say: 'I put this together for our family. You're listed as my healthcare decision-maker.' That's the whole conversation.

items.item: Tell someone where your Resolved Brief is kept
items.solution: Your brief is only useful if someone can find it. Tell one person: 'There's an envelope in [location]. If anything happens to me, start there.'
Set a calendar reminder. Open your brief, scan for anything that's changed. Most updates take 5 minutes.

follow_up_section.closing_message: You don't have to do it all today. You already did the hard part.
